original: Malala Yousafzaiand Kailash Satyarthi awarded the Nobel Peace Prize
Nordiques' Real Cloutier sets NHL record of a hat trick in 1st game
American chemist and anti-nuclear weapons campaignerLinus Paulingwins the Nobel Peace Prize the same day the first nuclear test treaty comes into effect
Amnesty International wins the Nobel Peace Prize, recognising its significant role in human rights activism
Harry Brook 317 and Joe Root 262 share highest ever partnership (454) by England on day 4 of 1st Test against Pakistan in Multan; fourth highest partnership of all-time in Test cricket
Louis Cyr, French Canadian strongman (lifting 500 pounds (227 kg) with one finger and backlifting 4,337 pounds (1,967 kg)), born in Quebec, Canada (d. 1912)
Earle Dickson, American inventor (Band-Aid), born in Grandview, Tennessee (d. 1961)
Jodi Benson, American voice actress (Ariel-The Little Mermaid), born in Rockford, Illinois
Greece's 98 year-old Dimitrion Yordanidis, is oldest man to compete in a marathon; he finishes in 7:33
Advisement to view a rhinoceros in a tavern in Ludgate Hill, London published - 1st rhinoceros in Europe since 1515
Neptune's moon Triton discovered by William Lassell
XVIII Summer Olympic Games open at the National Stadium in Tokyo, Japan
US 67th manned space mission STS 41 (Discovery 11) returns from space
Hurricane Michael makes landfall near Mexico Beach, Florida, as a category 4 hurricane with winds of 155 mph (250 km/h), going on to kill 27, having killed 15 in Central America
Rumiko Takahashi, Japanese artist, born in Niigata, Japan
Canadian bicyclist John Hathaway begins 2-year ride of 50,600 miles
The Beach Boys release their influential single "Good Vibrations"
Fiji gains independence from Britain (National Day)
Gamboa Dam in Panama blown up; Atlantic & Pacific waters mix
Thelonious Monk, American jazz pianist and composer ("Straight, No Chaser"; "'Round Midnight"; "Brilliant Corners"), born in Rocky Mount, North Carolina (d. 1982)
Cuba revolts for independence against Spain
African-American inventor Isaac R. Johnson patents the bicycle frame
Netherlands population hits 12,000,000
The Vinland Map is introduced by Yale University as the first known map of America, drawn around 1440
Panama assumes sovereignty over Canal Area (ie Canal Zone)
Very Large Array (VLA) radio telescope network dedicated
Garfield Park Nature Center opens, latest in Cleveland Metroparks
Hartford center Doug Jarvis ends his career having played an NHL record 964 consecutive games as the Whalers lose 6-2 to the New York Rangers
Nobel prize for physiology awarded to Alfred G. Gilman & Martin Rodbell for G-protein discoveries
3,500 women are the first to be allowed to attend a football match in Iran for a World Cup qualifier in Tehran, since the Islamic revolution
Italian teenage computer genius Carlo Acutis, who died of leukemia at 15, beatified by the Catholic Church in Assisi, Italy
Nobel prize for Literature awarded to South Korean author Han Kang
The Women's Social and Political Union is formed byEmmeline Pankhurstto fight for women's rights in Britain
US Congress approves dollar coin honoring women's suffrage campaignerSusan B. Anthony
PopeJohn Paul IIcanonizes RevMaximilian Kolbe, who volunteered to die in place of another inmate at Auschwitz concentration camp, a saint
Hungarian writer and Holocaust survivorImre Kertészis awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature
Robert LefkowitzandBrian Kobilkawin the 2012 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for work on G protein-coupled receptors
The Great Chicago Fire is finally extinguished after 3 days, leaving approximately 300 dead, 100,000 homeless, and costing $222m in damage
Henry Cavendish, England, physicist and chemist (discovered hydrogen), born in Nice (d. 1810)
Pete Mahvolich, Canadian ice hockey center (Stanley Cup 1971, 73, 76, 77 Montreal Canadiens; Canada Cup 1976), born in Timmins, Ontario
John M. Grunsfeld, American physicist and astronaut (STS 67, 81), born in Chicago, Illinois
Tanya Tucker, American Grammy Award-winning country singer-songwriter ("Delta Dawn"; "Bring Me Flowers Now"), born in Seminole, Texas
Han [Henricus Antonius] van Meegeren, Dutch painter and art forger (Vermeer), born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 1947)
Dnjepr Dam in USSR put into operation (world's biggest)
British progressive rock band King Crimson releases their debut album "In The Court Of The Crimson King"
British pop music magazine "Smash Hits" first published
Recorded trace of snow in Central Park NYC
"Giant", directed by George Stevens, starringElizabeth Taylor,Rock Hudson, andJames Deanin his last role, premieres in NYC
US PresidentEisenhowerapologizes to the finance minister of Ghana, Komla Agbeli Gbdemah, after he is refused service in a restaurant in Dover, Delaware
Comedy film "Private Benjamin" is released starringGoldie Hawn
Shirin Ebadiis awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for her significant and pioneering efforts for democracy and human rights, making her the 1st Iranian Muslim women to receive the prize
Ludmilla Tchérina, French prima ballerina, artist, actress, choreographer and author (Sins of Rome), born in Paris, France (d. 2004)
Gerhard Ertl, German surface chemist (Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2007), born in Stuttgart, Germany
James E. Ferguson II, American civil rights attorney known for his work on the desegregation of public schools, born in Asheville, North Carolina (d. 2025)[1]
James Marshall, American illustrator and author (George and Martha, The Stupids), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 1992)
Franco Malerba, Italian astronaut (STS 46), born in Genoa, Italy
(James) "Midge" Ure, Scottish singer-songwriter, guitarist, and synth player ("Dear God"; Ultravox - "We Came to Dance"; Band Aid - "Do They Know It's Christmas?"), born in Glasgow, Scotland
Victoria Clark, American actress (Kimberly Akimbo - Tony for Best Musical Actress 2023), born in Dallas, Texas
Rex J. Walheim, U.S. Air Force officer and NASA astronaut, born in Redwood City, California
Anita Mui, Hong Kong Cantopop singer and actress, "Madonna of the East", born in Hong Kong (d. 2003)
